topic: Context 3: School Life, Studies and the World of Work
subject: spanish

items.q: What is the difference between 'el trabajo' and 'los deberes'?
items.correct: El trabajo means work or a job; los deberes means homework.
items.misconception: Trabajo is the word for homework because homework is work.
items.why: Students map 'homework = work' directly; Spanish separates paid or general work (trabajo) from school homework (deberes), and exams test both.

items.q: What can 'la carrera' mean in the context of studies and work?
items.correct: A university degree course or a career (as well as a race): 'estudiar la carrera de medicina'.
items.misconception: La carrera only means a running race.
items.random_wrong: La carrera means the school canteen.
items.why: In texts about future plans, 'carrera' almost always means degree or career; the sport-only reading makes such passages incomprehensible.

items.q: How do you say 'I want to be a doctor' in Spanish?
items.correct: 'Quiero ser médico' - no article before the profession.
items.misconception: 'Quiero ser un médico', adding 'un' as in English.
items.why: Spanish omits the indefinite article with professions after ser; inserting 'un/una' is a direct transfer from English and a very common error in work-related writing.
The answer examiners want is: Me gusta el español.. “Me gusto el español.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which sentence means 'Maths is more difficult than English'?
items.correct: Las matemáticas son más difíciles que el inglés.
items.misconception: Las matemáticas es más difícil que el inglés.
items.random_wrong: Las matemáticas son menos difíciles que el inglés.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Las matemáticas son más difíciles que el inglés.. “Las matemáticas es más difícil que el inglés.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How do you say 'I study English at school' in Spanish?
items.random_wrong: Estudio el inglés a la escuela.
items.why: Correct answer: Estudio inglés en el colegio.. “Yo estudia inglés en escuela.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How do you describe weather during a past holiday: 'It was sunny'?
items.why: Correct answer: Imperfect - 'Hacía sol' (descriptive ongoing state).. “Present - 'Hace sol'.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “El horario”?
items.correct: Timetable: el horario, el recreo (break), el comedor (canteen), los deberes (homework), las clases.
items.misconception: más...que (more than), menos...que (less than), tan...como (as...as). 'Es más difícil que el inglés.'
items.random_wrong: School subjects: las matemáticas, el español, el inglés, la historia, la geografía, las ciencias, la educación física.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Timetable: el horario, el recreo (break), el comedor (canteen), los deberes (homework), las clases.. “más...que (more than), menos...que (less than), tan...como (as...as). 'Es más difícil que el inglés.'” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “El uniforme”?
items.correct: Uniform vocab: el uniforme, llevar (to wear), una falda, una camisa, un jersey, una corbata.
items.misconception: 'Gustar' works like 'to be pleasing to': 'Me gusta el español' (Spanish is pleasing to me). Plural: 'Me gustan las matemáticas.'
items.random_wrong: Opinions: 'Me gusta...' (I like), 'Odio...' (I hate), 'Es interesante / aburrido / útil / difícil'.
items.why: Correct answer: Uniform vocab: el uniforme, llevar (to wear), una falda, una camisa, un jersey, una corbata.. “'Gustar' works like 'to be pleasing to': 'Me gusta el español' (Spanish is pleasing to me). Plural: 'Me gustan las matemáticas.'” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: más...que (more than), menos...que (less than), tan...como (as...as). 'Es más difícil que el inglés.'
items.misconception: Timetable: el horario, el recreo (break), el comedor (canteen), los deberes (homework), las clases.
items.random_wrong: School subjects: las matemáticas, el español, el inglés, la historia, la geografía, las ciencias, la educación física.

Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many subject pronouns does Spanish have (yo, tú, él/ella/usted, nosotros, vosotros, ellos/ellas/ustedes)?
items.why: Correct answer: 6 pronouns. “5 pronouns” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
